posts.name: Andrew Christiansen

Sold a couple cases of them last year, so I’ll never know. Proceeds were used on a few bottles of Bartolo and Jamet, so I’m happy how they turned out.

Honesty, I fell prey to the hype and bought more than I should have, since I found the vintage to be too homogenous and dense for my tastes. Really enjoyed several 2007’s and 2008’s, but they seemed to fall apart by about 2012. I bailed on 2009 while they still had some value.

posts.name: Richard Malloy
posts.cooked:
After drinking '09s like crazy over the first couple years following release, I’ve managed to keep my hands off most of my stash over the last year. In general, I find that these are beyond their early drinking stage, and while not really ready are also not really shutdown - rather going through something akin to adolescent awkwardness. Still, I drunk a few over the last six months. Didn’t write notes for all unfortunately, but will try to recall some impressions on those:

2009 Jean-Marc Burgaud Morgon Côte du Py Vieilles Vignes - 10/31/2013: Wasn’t paying close attention, but recall black cherry, spice, a bit brooding with more substance than many other semi-carbonic bojos. These have been tight since release. Hold.

2009 Jean Foillard Morgon Côte du Py - 10/30/2013: A reticent nose of faint dark fruit, florals, and pine is a bit more pronounced on the palate. The initial ripeness of the fruit has begun to fade and the stemmy notes are more to the fore creating an intense inner perfume of pine needles and peppery spice. Silken on the palate, but with a meaty intensity reinforced by unresolved tannin and a good bit of extract on the finish. Very structured and still needing a longer decant than I was able to give it tonight. There is more complexity here now, even though the overall expression of the wine is more restrained than in its flamboyant youth. It’s been about a year since my last bottle, and it seems this wine has just begun to turn the corner into a more mature profile. Give it a good decant or hold.

2009 Domaine des Terres Dorées (Jean-Paul Brun) Moulin-à-Vent - 9/27/2013: Drank over two nights. Very tight and unyielding on night one, and revealing itself only a bit on night two. Nose of smoke, pepper, black-fruit, and pine needles. Dark and brooding on the palate, unclenching on night two just enough to show tart black fruit, pepper, iron, and beef jus. Finish is a bit clipped with astringent tannins, and loads of dry extract. Like the 2009 Coudert Cuvee Tardive, this wine is still much too young, and I regret opening this bottle. Gilman’s ambitious drinking window may well prove correct (2015-2050). Hold.

2009 Marcel Lapierre Morgon - 5/22/2013: Recall it being a bit more mature on the nose than the prior bottle, losing some of those wonderful whole-cluster florals. In the mouth, silky and soft showing gentle dark red fruit and baking spice. That first of youth has definitely faded, the invigorating midpalate lift and intensity of fruit and spice is dialed back, but a more mature autumnal quality seems starting to emerge. Drink with a good decant or hold.

I finished up the last of my base Couderts last summer - delicious as I recall but no note. After drinking 7 bottles of the 09 Cuvee Tardive at a mercilessly, ridiculously, what-was-I-thinking young stage over the first couple years following release, I’m proud to say I drank not a single one in 2013 (or '14 so far)! I also managed to avoid other early drinking casualties among a very dwindling stash of '09 Vissoux/Chermette Moulin-à-Vent Les Trois Roches and Fleurie Les Garants (another couple of my favorites of the vintage).

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked: The 06 St. Jacques was beginning to drink nicely a year or so ago, if that helps you to calibrate at all.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
This thread piqued my curiosity. I was wondering in particular how much of this was sort of the AFWE / WB disdain for “ripe” vintages like 2009, versus how much of this was applicable to those of us who rather like ripe vintages in France.

Anyway, my one data point here won’t answer that question, but I popped a 2009 Jean-Claude Lapalu Brouilly Croix des Rameaux. This just leaps out of the gate. Huge nose of sweet cherry and mint, mixed with some earthy funk in the background. The palate is not as savory as other Bojos, but it offers hints of fruit pit and mineral, and some grapey acids on the finish.

Really, the main impression from this wine is of the juicy, ripe red berry fruit. This is like a big puppy following you around wagging its tail joyously with a whimsical chew toy in its mouth. This pulled back slightly after the first few sips, but it still stayed very open.

I haven’t had other vintages of this wine, so I don’t know how much is the house style and how much is the ripe 2009 vintage. But an enjoyable glass of wine, if more uncomplicated and overtly pleasurable than cerebral.

[A decent amount of sediment already in this wine, so it’s one to stand up and pour carefully or decant, just FYI.]

** After two hours of being open, I’m finishing the first half of the bottle. This has substantially firmed up about an hour after opening. It becomes more chalky and taut, and the sappy red fruit is now in the background. The funk has also fully blown off. I think it was more fun in the early going, but it’s still pretty decent now.

I can see how this might be one of those “this is why I don’t like the 2009 vintage” wines, and I respect that point of view, but I think this is a wine that is quite happy to drink, if a simpler pleasure.

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
Chris Seiber:

2009 Jean-Claude Lapalu Brouilly Croix des Rameaux.

…

I haven’t had other vintages of this wine, so I don’t know how much is the house style and how much is the ripe 2009 vintage.

Lapalu is known for a ripe style and I found his wines usually quite opulent as well (in any case: very tasty). It’s probably a mix of house style and vintage in this case.

The last few 2009 Beaujolais Crus I drank (Château de Bellevue - Morgon Le Clos, Domaine Labruyère - Moulin-à-Vent Grande Cuvée, Thibault Liger-Belair - Moulin-à-Vent) were drinkable, but seemed not quite ready to go. I am very confident that most 2009s will be marvellous once they have the proper drinking age. From what the producers say, all you could do wrong in 2009 was pick too late (as displayed for example in the Fleurie “1889” by Jean-Marc Deprès).

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked: Have you had the Thibault Liger-Belair Beaujolais in other vintages? How do you like them?

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
Ian Fitzsimmons:

Have you had the Thibault Liger-Belair Beaujolais in other vintages? How do you like them?

So far, I only had the Moulin-à-Vent Vieilles Vignes from 2009 and 2010. Actually, I liked both vintages equally well. The 2009 is rounder, fatter and fruitier while the 2010 is a tiny bit leaner and a tiny bit less expressive, but it’s a bit clearer in its aromatics. 2011 is the first vintage where - with a bit of luck - I managed to score 3 bottles of the Moulin-à-Vent Vignes Centenaires, but I haven’t seen the “Rocheaux” and the “La Roche” in Germany so far.

The style is definitely more Thibault than Moulin-à-Vent I’d say, I drank the 2010 side by side with the Bourgogne Grands Chaillots and the Hautes Côtes de Nuits “Clos du Prieuré” from the same vintage and the Moulin-à-Vent resembled the two Pinot Noirs quite a bit. Thibault destemms completely, there’s no maceration carbonique or semi-carbonique and the wines age in pièces. I’d say, it’s recognizable that it’s a Moulin-à-Vent, but it’s less typical for the appelation than other domaines using Burgundian vinification methods (such as e.g. Ch. des Jacques, Ch. du Moulin-à-Vent, Domaine Labruyère). Maybe the typicity will come with more bottle age.

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
Thank you. Moulin by reputation can be a cellar-worthy wine, and if the T L-B guys are vinifying in the Burgundian style, without carbonique, I wouldn’t expect them to show their true colors until after the passage of some years - just as with Desvignes and the Jadot St. Jacques line.

I’ve walked around the T L-B Beaujolais up till now, because of pricing, but should probably have a look at them.

Drinking very well. A fine producer.

Did you decant or otherwise aerate?

I chill my Bojos and pop and pour. I never aerate or decant Bojos. Wine is spelled Corcelette. I went back and reviewed my recent 09 Bojo tasting notes and saw that I didn’t care very much for the Foillard Morgon Cote du Py. The later gets mixed scores in CT, but I like it very much.

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked: Not to change the subject, but as much as I bought in 2009, I bought more in 2011.

Not to inspire thread drift but, from a tasting perspective, how would I tell whether a wine has undergone carbonic maceration vs the more Burgundian treatment? Was able to taste a phenomenal 2012 Metras MaV and was just floored by it. What else would compare (for a more typically Bojo price)?

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
NoahR:

Not to inspire thread drift but, from a tasting perspective, how would I tell whether a wine has undergone carbonic maceration vs the more Burgundian treatment? Was able to taste a phenomenal 2012 Metras MaV and was just floored by it. What else would compare (for a more typically Bojo price)?

Other than maceration carbonique and Burgundian vinification, there’s also the maceration semi-carbonique which seems to be applied by most producers, where part of the grapes are pressed and start fermentation before whole grapes are added.

I’d say that you can identify wines made with maceration carbonique by their typically light colour, their fresh fruit character and their grapiness whereas the wines made with Burgundian vinification are typically a bit meatier, fuller and less direct, but that’s also because many Beaujolais which are made entirely with destemmed grapes and a short cold-soak often are aged in pièces, sometimes also some proportion of new barrels, so that definitely has an influence as well.

Unfortunately, I haven’t found a real substitute for Mêtras, but for me wines that come close to Yvon Mêtras’ wines are those from Jean-Louis Dutraive (Domaine de la Grand Cour), the Fleurie Grille Midi from Jean-Paul Brun, the Fleurie from Jean Foillard and - to a lesser extent - the Fleurie and Regnié from Julien Sunier.
Steven - thanks!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Interesting, Steven, thanks. Had not really thought of the wine-making technique, but since you point it out, the meatier Beaujolais are my preferred style as well. Where do you place Thivin?

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Where do you place Thivin?

Hi Robert,

Thivin is very traditional in its winemaking. They vinify by maceration semi-carbonique in concrete tanks. Then, the wines age in foudre casks except for the Cuvée Zacharie which is aged in used barriques. There are not that many producers who do a full maceration carbonique (the ones I know of are Marcel/Mathieu Lapierre, Yvon Mêtras, Jean Foillard, Julien Sunier for some wines, Nicole Chanrion, Jean-Louis Dutraive and some others). I can think of only very few who do the Burgundian vinification, it’s often those who come from Burgundy or have ties to Burgund: Thibault Liger Belair, Château des Jacques (Louis Jadot), Château du Moulin-à-Vent, Clos de MEZ (Marie Elodie Confuron-Zighera is the wife of Yves Confuron of Domaine Confuron-Cotedidot), I think also Potel-Avyron plus some domaines for some of their wines.

The most common vinification method to me seems to be maceration semi-carbonique and I feel like it’s harder for those wines to say that they have a certain aromatic or characteristic profile. It all depends on producer, site, vintage, details of elevage, etc.

posts.name: Peter Kleban
posts.cooked:
StevenB:
NoahR:

Not to inspire thread drift but, from a tasting perspective, how would I tell whether a wine has undergone carbonic maceration vs the more Burgundian treatment? Was able to taste a phenomenal 2012 Metras MaV and was just floored by it. What else would compare (for a more typically Bojo price)?

Other than maceration carbonique and Burgundian vinification, there’s also the maceration semi-carbonique which seems to be applied by most producers, where part of the grapes are pressed and start fermentation before whole grapes are added.

I’d say that you can identify wines made with maceration carbonique by their typically light colour, their fresh fruit character and their grapiness whereas the wines made with Burgundian vinification are typically a bit meatier, fuller and less direct, but that’s also because many Beaujolais which are made entirely with destemmed grapes and a short cold-soak often are aged in pièces, sometimes also some proportion of new barrels, so that definitely has an influence as well.

Unfortunately, I haven’t found a real substitute for Mêtras, but for me wines that come close to Yvon Mêtras’ wines are those from Jean-Louis Dutraive (Domaine de la Grand Cour), the Fleurie Grille Midi from Jean-Paul Brun, the Fleurie from Jean Foillard and - to a lesser extent - the Fleurie and Regnié from Julien Sunier.

Have you tried Balagny? Very similar, in my limited experience (one bottle of '11 Fleurie “La Carioca” which I liked a lot). She was his student; her wines are cheaper.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
This was written by a Finnish student in his 2013 blog post about Julie Balagny…dovetails with my feelings on the subject.

“She looked like the weather that day: stormy. And violently passionate…when she asked us about our wine preferences, I told her I preferred the lighter, less ripe vintages in Beaujolais like 2008, 2010 and very possibly 2012 and wasn’t so keen on such over-ripe years as 2009. She jumped about ten meters into the air (I’m not lying!) and shouted “YEAHHHHHH!” (or was that a thunderclap?) and finally she smiled.”

RT
posts.display_username: Richard T r i m p i

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
Richard T r i m p i:

“She looked like the weather that day: stormy. And violently passionate…when she asked us about our wine preferences, I told her I preferred the lighter, less ripe vintages in Beaujolais like 2008, 2010 and very possibly 2012 and wasn’t so keen on such over-ripe years as 2009. She jumped about ten meters into the air (I’m not lying!) and shouted “YEAHHHHHH!” (or was that a thunderclap?) and finally she smiled.”

Thanks for the quote. I’m always a bit sceptical when I read things like that. There’s a thin line between ripe and over-ripe and out of the roughly 60 or 70 2009 Beaujolais I’ve tasted and drunk, there were maybe 2 or 3 wines which I found over-ripe, none which seemed underripe and the rest felt ripe on the riper side of the spectrum, but with vibrant, not jammy, cooked or dried fruit. Soft tannins and mild acidity, yes. But for me, the 2009s are not overripe.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
StevenB:

But for me, the 2009s are not overripe.

Steven, I find it’s a “relativity” thing. 09 Bojos have been and continue to be, in general, riper than my preference. Same is true for Burgundy. But the differences are highlighted by comparisons to the 08s, 10s and 11s (just enjoyed an '11 Chamonard Morgon Clos de Lys last night). It’s possible that time will bring them back in to my wheelhouse. The differences are minimized when you broaden the comparisons to any of a plethora of hotter, riper wines and regions (i.e.: Southern Rhone, Languedoc/Roussillon).

RT
posts.display_username: Richard T r i m p i

posts.name: Len Fox
posts.cooked: I don’t find 09 to be over ripe, but it is certainly an atypical vintage. I’ve found the wines to be bigger - denser, greater concentration- and far more blue fruited than I prefer and a little short on acidity,but I don’t get over ripe or plushness. I remember 05 being a bigger vintage but not as blue fruited and having more structure than 09. The 05’s are drinking very well now. The 09’s will not become 05’s, but I think with time will shed this awkwardness and will reward patience.

posts.name: John Danza
posts.cooked:
I don’t think the 09s are over ripe either, but they certainly have a profile that isn’t typically seen in the “average” Bojo vintage (whatever that is). They have a complexity that I don’t typically find, almost like a Burgundian component combined with the Gamay fruitiness. I think they’ll rival the 05s but still have a different profile.

Fortunately I bought enough to track their development over a 10-15 year period. But if I’m wrong, I’ll be dumping a lot of the contents of my cellar!

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked:
Visited an '09 Lapierre the last two nights. Very enjoyable.

  • 2009 Marcel Lapierre Morgon - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Morgon (6/22/2014)
    A fleshy but not a flabby Morgon. Fresh red fruit and berries plus a distinct savory, earthy herbal note. Nice on P&P, better on day two.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Had an 2009 Thibault Liger Belair moulin a vent la roche this evening. Has an absolutely knockout nose of bright red fruit and just the slightest background hint of funk. Palate seemed to change by the minute though and I couldn’t figure it out. One point it’s very acidic and tart red cherry, next sip fleshes out and picks up more balance. Tertiary notes on the awesome nose but not yet on the palate. Wondering if I missed the window or wait for more palate development.

Edit to add that on day 2 this really fleshed out with some body and fruit, coming across more balanced. acid heads would certainly dig this though.

posts.name: Steve Brickley
posts.cooked:
I went a little goofy on this one about a year ago. I’ll have to check back in. I was surprised that six of them showed up on auction without other bids so I bought them back in late 2016 . No regrets.

  • 2009 Marcel Lapierre Morgon - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Morgon (12/19/2016)
    Barnyard funk on the nose just beautiful and borrows from a red burgundy. Can I describe a beaujolais as a long finish? The initial attack is light, but mid and lasting long palate just keeps bringing back unsweetened black cherry. This gamay has it all and is just singing and light on its feet. Acids in check and could give this years and years of life. Lovely. (96 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker
Steve, awesome note on an amazing wine. I have one 750 remaining and will open it in 2019.

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
Vissoux Moulin-a-Vent Trois Roches, excellent: the initial generous fruit now nicely in balance with the acidity. Seems ready.

Vissoux Fleurie Garants (a few months ago), likewise excellent, in a different profile - less juiciness, more verve & pizzazz.

Coudertl Vendage Tardive (a few months ago), coming around nicely, no rush.

Regretting now that I failed to buy these cuvées in 2014. Dang.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: I had a Dominique Piron - Morgon - Cotes du Py tonight that was somewhat oxidized – well past its prime. The friends who served it said other bottles recently had been good, though, so perhaps this was an anomoly. The Jules Desjourneys - Fleurie - Chapelle des Bois they opened as a replacement was very good.

posts.name: P@ul Int@g
posts.cooked:
So I popped one of the 3 2009 Lapierre Morgon’s that I just got from Vins Rare. I don’t think it was a completely sound bottle, but I have no experience with Cru Bojo with any age on them. I found it to be slightly oxidized and heavy with a little VA. Still drinkable, but not a great experience. I’ll try another bottle soon.

  • 2009 Marcel Lapierre Morgon - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Morgon (3/1/2018)
    Ever so slightly oxidized and with a touch of VA. No brett that I could detect. Seemed heavy, despite the abundant acidity. Still enjoyable, but not a great bottle. The oldest bottle of Cru Bojo I have tried up to this point, so not much of a reference point. I hope my remaining 2 bottle show better.

posts.name: Todd Cavallo
posts.cooked:
All the (S only) Lapierre 09 Morgons I have opened have showed beautifully, and there have been quite a few from three different sources over the years.

Last night popped a different 09 to go with taco night:

  • 2009 Coudert Fleurie Clos de la Roilette - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Fleurie (3/6/2018)
    Drinking perfectly right now. Opens up with bright red raspberry and cherry fruit and a darker undertone of violet and moist earth. Rich and round mid-palate with some earthiness carried over from the nose. Herbaceous finish with ample structure remaining to give this another 5-10 years easily. (92 pts.)

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
Josh Grossman:

I apologize that this isn’t in regards to '09 ('15 instead) and don’t mean to thread drift. I came to realize that I had 400 bottles of wine in my cellar and hardly any that were ‘ready to drink’. I went heavy on '15 Single Cru Beaujolais in order to rectify this. This includes, Daniel Bouland Morgon Corcelette Vieilles Vignes, Daniel Bouland Morgon Delys, Daniel Bouland Morgon Schiste, Coudert Fleurie Clos de la Roilette Cuvée Tardive, Jean Foillard Morgon Côte du Py, Jean Foillard Morgon Cuvée Corcelette, Domaine De Robert Fleurie Cuvée Tradition, and Château Thivin Côte de Brouilly Cuvée Zaccharie. I’ve made a terrible mistake.

Josh - you have wines from some exceptional producers there. I didn’t buy much in 2015, but did in 2009. I’m not sure why you think you’ve made a mistake, but my suggestion would be to be patient: Gamay is such a high-acid grape that even those wines with what seems like a ton of fruit in their youth will often come around to a good balance. The 2009 Foillard Cote de Py is a case in point, as is the Roilette CT - both are just beginning to enter their drinking windows now, iMO.

My observation regarding aging Beaujolais, however, is that the good ones sometimes go through a period of transition from youthful fruitiness to shut-down, when they taste completely awful, as if they’ve gone bad. I’ve come to think this is a phase in which the abundant youthful fruit is essentially decomposing. Because of the great acidity, however, in these cases, the wines can come through to show strikingly different character than in their youth, with great, lacy acid backbone supporting refined fruit flavors at varying stages of maturity. These wines from this point on can be not just good but tremendous.

Not a sure thing that your 2015s will travel this route, but I’d urge you to give them the chance. Read the cellar tracker notes for Foillard’s 2009s to get a sense of the time that elapsed from vintage until really glowing reviews begin to pop up (in this case, just during the last year or so).

If you decide to unload your Thivin, on the other hand, pls drop me a line

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
Peter Kleban:

Lapierre MMVII Morgon is even better then last night! OMG is this lovely.

I swept up a parcel of MMXIV just last week, in contradiction of my current ‘no-buy’ policy. The only time I’ve tasted the cuvée Marcel (MMIX, I think, imbibed in its youth), it was a thing of crystalline wonder.

Fingers crossed.

posts.name: Mark C Johnson

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:

  • 2009 Marcel Lapierre Morgon - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Morgon (3/1/2018)
    Ever so slightly oxidized and with a touch of VA. No brett that I could detect. Seemed heavy, despite the abundant acidity. Still enjoyable, but not a great bottle. The oldest bottle of Cru Bojo I have tried up to this point, so not much of a reference point. I hope my remaining 2 bottle show better.

Do you know if this was a no-sulpher bottle? Lapierre’s 09 should be in good shape now (maybe young), but the sans soufre bottling could easily have different aging characteristics.

I found several bottles of '09 to be so highly flawed by MLF bacteria and brett that they were outright faulty; they tasted exactly like sour beer. These were bought from a store that’s kept very cool just after release. We had something similar happen at the store with several '15s where I work. They were getting fizzy and definitely tasted flawed. This doesn’t sound like what Paul is describing, but variability with this wine, especially in warm vintages, shouldn’t be surprising. The problems I saw were all with the “S” bottling.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
StevenB:
NoahR:

Not to inspire thread drift but, from a tasting perspective, how would I tell whether a wine has undergone carbonic maceration vs the more Burgundian treatment? Was able to taste a phenomenal 2012 Metras MaV and was just floored by it. What else would compare (for a more typically Bojo price)?

Other than maceration carbonique and Burgundian vinification, there’s also the maceration semi-carbonique which seems to be applied by most producers, where part of the grapes are pressed and start fermentation before whole grapes are added.

I’m not a fan of calling that ‘partial carbonic’ because I find that that term is over-used and somewhat disingenuous. To me, the only way that a wine would be ‘partially carbonic’ is if they did multiple fermentations, with at least one done with traditional carbonic techniques and the others not and then blended together.

Many winemakers are not calling their wines ‘partial carbonic’ because they do not crush all of their grapes - but they are doing open top ferments, perhaps pumping over, etc. That’s not ‘partial carbonic’ to me - it’s whole cluster with uncrushed clusters . . .

Cheers.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Had a couple nice ones this past week:

2009 Lapierre cuvée Marcel Lapierre MMIX - my first go at this wine and imo this is really not a classic Beaujolais experience, but is certainly an impressive wine. The concentration and dark fruit depth are quite amazing for the region but not overly done in the sense of big wines in general. No way I’d pick this out blind though, I’d probably guess medium weight Syrah or something? This will go another 10-15 years easy as it didn’t show much development but was certainly approachable now. This bottle was clean which relieved my anxiety a bit about my other bottles.

2009 Domaine des Terres Dorées (Jean-Paul Brun) Morgon - the vintage is showing a bit here as well but this is definitely identifiable right off as bojo. Lots of dark cherry fruit with a bit of that zip and crunch to keep it lively. The nose here is a knockout of pure cherry. This is aging nicely and showing some development on the palate and is ready to drink but also in no hurry, I’d say now to 5-7 more years if you want more secondary flavor. A bit of tannin does build as the bottle goes down. Really a pleasure though for the $18 I paid.

posts.name: Steve Brickley
posts.cooked:
Robert,

I thoroughly enjoyed the 2009 Lapierre Morgon enough to backfill what I could find. I agree it wasn’t the typical beaujolais. I, too, have hope for my remaining bottles to hold up. Nice note of yours, thank you.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked: Steve this was the Cuvee Marcel Lapierre MMIX not sure if you noticed that and I’ve updated post because maybe I wasnt clear enough. I’ve enjoyed many of the regular over the years too though (although I’ve had about 1/3 bretty) and hope to continue to do so for years to come.

participants.name: Richard T r i m p i
Glenn L e v i n e
